problem_summary: 2012 MCM/ICM Problem C (Interdisciplinary): identify the participants in a corporate conspiracy from a network of 400 message headers (sender-receiver pairs) among 83 communication nodes (~82 employees), where messages carry only 15 topic labels (3 known suspicious) — no full text, no timestamps. Given 7 known conspirators and 8 known non-conspirators, plus an undetermined eighth person (Chris) and an undetermined Topic 1, the task is to rank the remaining employees by likelihood of conspiracy involvement, resolve data ambiguities (5 duplicate names, one corrupt topic ID), and specifically assess three senior managers (Gretchen, Jerome, Dolores). Team 17160 framed it as a generic "agent-ranking by network proximity to known-positive vs known-negative anchors" problem.
models_used: Electric network / resistor-circuit model (MAIN): a bipartite graph G=(N,T,E) of node-vertices and topic-vertices; edges carry conductance = number of co-occurring communications. Known conspirators and suspicious topics fixed at 1V, known innocents grounded at 0V; unknown vertices' voltages solved as a DC steady-state linear system (Kirchhoff/Laplacian solve). Output voltage in [0,1] = 'guilt level' ranking.

Vector space model: each of 83 nodes mapped to a length-98 vector (83 node-node interaction counts + 15 node-topic counts), Euclidean norm as distance — shared exactly with the circuit's conductance matrix C so the two models co-validate.

k-means clustering (AUXILIARY): clustering the 83 row-vectors of C into k=2..5 clusters with Euclidean distance, run repeatedly to find the most probable (stable) partition since k-means is a local optimum of an NP-hard objective.
Random-walk / electric-network equivalence (Doyle & Snell) used as theoretical justification that the circuit subsumes PageRank-like Markov methods without the row-normalization assumption.
Leave-one-out robustness analysis (re-solving the circuit 10 times, each dropping one of the 7 known conspirators or 3 suspicious topics from the fixed-voltage set).
Historical base-rate model for the investigate/don't-investigate cutoff (FBI 2009 corporate-fraud statistics → ~20% upper bound → top-17 cutoff).
key_techniques: Casting nodes and topics as a single bipartite graph and treating communication counts as electrical conductance, so 'closeness to guilt' becomes solvable steady-state voltage — an elegant reduction of an ill-posed ranking problem to a well-posed linear system.

Dirichlet boundary conditions: pinning known-guilty/suspicious vertices to 1V and known-innocent to 0V, letting the harmonic solution interpolate guilt over unknowns (this is effectively a harmonic-function / semi-supervised label-propagation solve, framed in circuit language).

Deliberate parameter minimization to fight overfitting — they reduce the model to a SINGLE effective free parameter (the α/β sent-vs-received weight ratio, set to 1), explicitly computing that a 3-parameter grid (1331 settings) over a 10-person training example (1024 guilt combinations) would spuriously fit by chance.

Two mutually-supporting models: the auxiliary k-means clustering is used not to rank but to JUSTIFY the circuit's modeling choices — Table 2 shows people-to-topic (p2t) connections cluster the known-guilty best (7 of 7 guilty in one cluster, 0 in the other), validating the decision to drop node-to-node edges.

Using clustering to resolve data ambiguity: deciding which of 5 duplicate names is one person (cluster co-membership across k=2..5 plus the 'people don't message themselves' heuristic → the two Beths are merged) and which Elsie node is the known conspirator.

Leave-one-out testing explicitly motivated by a legal concern (avoiding 'guilt by association' / probable-cause contamination), not just statistics — 6 people stay in the top-10 across all 10 leave-one-out runs.
Sensitivity / scenario analysis: re-running both the Chris-innocent and Chris-guilty (Topic-1 suspicious) cases and showing 12 of top-15 are conserved.
Base-rate anchoring of the decision threshold using real FBI conviction statistics rather than an arbitrary cutoff.
why_it_won: "From a judge's perspective this paper wins on modeling sophistication married to disciplined restraint. The electric-network formulation is genuinely clever and non-obvious — it reframes a fuzzy social-network 'who is guilty' question as a clean DC circuit solve, and the team backs it with the Doyle-Snell random-walk equivalence to argue it dominates the PageRank-style approaches most teams would have reached for, while avoiding PageRank's unjustified normalization. Crucially, they don't just show off: the entire paper is organized around an explicit 'approach philosophy' (avoid overfitting, avoid presuming guilt in intermediate terms, guard against guilt-by-association) that they then honor at every step. They quantify the overfitting risk with an actual counting argument, drive the model down to ONE free parameter, and use an independent k-means model purely to validate their assumptions rather than to pad the page count. The robustness story is unusually complete for a 4-day contest: leave-one-out over both people and topics, a merged-vs-unmerged name sensitivity test, and a two-scenario (Chris guilty/innocent) comparison, all showing the top suspects are stable. They engage seriously with the interdisciplinary/legal framing (admissibility, probable cause, guilt by association) which is exactly what Problem C's 'crime ring for a DA' context rewards. Finally the generalization section (same circuit retargeted to find infected cells in a biological network) demonstrates the transferable thinking judges prize. It is creative, self-aware about its own weaknesses, validated, and decision-relevant — the full Outstanding profile."
section_highlights: "Section 2 'Approach Philosophy' is the standout structural move — stating three guiding principles (anti-overfitting with a concrete combinatorial argument, no presumed guilt in intermediate terms, guard against guilt-by-association) up front turns the rest of the paper into a defense of those principles and signals maturity immediately. The abstract is dense and self-contained: it names both models, the 1V/0V boundary conditions, the leave-one-out test, and the strengths/weaknesses, so a judge knows the whole story in one paragraph. Section 5.1 'Introduction and Motivation' is strong because it justifies the model choice theoretically (Doyle-Snell equivalence, triangle inequality, expressiveness vs simplicity) before any equations. Section 5.4 explicitly and honestly lists weaknesses — e.g. a node with one suspicious message gets pinned to 1V and tops the ranking spuriously (Dayi). Section 6 separates immediate results, robustness, and the cutoff decision cleanly, and Section 6.3 grounds the threshold in FBI base rates. The clear notation block (tx(m), rx(m), topics(m), refs(t), indicator I_S) and the boxed final conductance formula make the math reproducible. Section 5.3 (generalization to a biological cell-infection network) and Section 7 (future work: LSI/WordNet semantic topic similarity, sentiment analysis for node-to-node edges) show extensibility. The full 82-employee ranked appendix gives the DA an actionable, prioritized list."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Boundary nodes get pinned artifacts: a node whose only message touches a suspicious topic is forced to exactly 1V and tops the ranking despite near-zero evidence (Dayi: one message, Sheng) — the team flags this honestly but it means the headline ranking over-weights sparsely-connected nodes. The 318/400 unique sender-receiver pairs make per-edge frequency essentially unusable.

The model has no principled guilt cutoff; the 20%/top-17 threshold is borrowed from FBI corporate-fraud base rates, which is reasonable but external to the model and not derived from the voltages themselves.

By construction the bipartite circuit discards all node-to-node edges, so direct person-to-person communication structure (sentiment, who-talks-to-whom) is thrown away — justified for anti-overfitting but a real information loss they acknowledge.

Validation is necessarily soft: only 7 known guilty and 8 known innocent anchors exist, so 'it clusters/ranks the known-guilty high' is a weak ground-truth check (and leave-one-out recovers only 3 of 7 — Yao, Alex, Ulf — into the top-10 when dropped).

The duplicate-name resolution leans on a heuristic ('people don't message themselves' + cluster co-membership that is itself unstable across k, e.g. Table 1 shows Gretchen/Neal flipping), so the manager attribution (which Jerome/Gretchen is the senior manager) is an inference the team itself says needs a real company directory to confirm.
k-means is a local, randomized optimum; they mitigate by re-running but the reported clusters can vary, weakening any single-run claim.
No timestamps used at all, so temporal escalation / ordering of the conspiracy is entirely outside the model.
Topic ID 18 (a corrupt entry) was simply discarded rather than recovered.
reusable_patterns: The 'electric network / harmonic label-propagation' template: cast any 'rank unknown agents by proximity to known-good vs known-bad anchors' problem as a conductance-weighted graph, pin anchors to 1/0, solve the linear (Laplacian) system, read voltages as scores. Directly reusable for influence, infection-spread, risk-propagation, recommendation, and trust-ranking problems.

Bipartite agent–attribute graph: when direct agent-agent links are noisy/sparse, route relationships through shared attributes (here topics) — attribute co-membership implicitly reconstructs agent similarity while removing parameters.
Lead with an explicit 'Approach Philosophy' section stating 2–4 principles, then justify every modeling decision against them — a powerful narrative spine that reads as judgment and maturity.

Quantify the overfitting risk with a concrete combinatorial count (model-search-space size vs number of distinguishable training outcomes) and use it to justify minimizing free parameters — far more persuasive than asserting 'we kept it simple'.

Pair a simple MAIN model with an independent AUXILIARY model whose only job is to validate the main model's assumptions/metrics (here k-means co-validating the circuit's distance metric and its drop of node-node edges).

Leave-one-out over each fixed/anchor input as a robustness test, and explicitly tie it to a domain concern (guilt-by-association / probable cause) so it reads as both statistical rigor and contextual relevance.
Run the genuinely ambiguous inputs both ways (Chris guilty vs innocent) and report what's conserved vs what shuffles, turning data ambiguity into a sensitivity-analysis strength.
Anchor a decision threshold in real-world base rates (cite domain statistics) instead of an arbitrary cutoff.

Include a 'generalization' section showing the same model retargeted to a different domain (crime ring → biological infected-cell detection) to demonstrate transferable structure — a recurring trait of Outstanding papers.
Provide a clean math notation block and a boxed final formula plus a full ranked appendix so results are reproducible and immediately actionable for the stakeholder (the DA).
